item.topic: the silence after a long argument

haiku_bare.text:
After a long argument.

Your back turned to the stove.
My hands grip the edge of the kitchen table—
the one with the burn mark from two summers ago,
the match we forgot about.

The refrigerator hums.
Outside, a car alarm lasts nine seconds.

You said: I don't know who you are anymore.
I said: I know.

Your silence now is not empty, but full:
full of the things you didn't say,
the ways you could have fought harder,
the apologies we won't make.

Twenty minutes have passed.
My coffee has cooled to room temperature.
A thin skin forms on its surface.

I could reach across the table.
The distance is the length of two forearms.
Your back. My watching.
The fan circulates warm air that solves nothing.

This is what remains:
the hum of the refrigerator,
the street below,
anything but our voices.

not weightless, not transparent,
but the specific dust of everything we burned.

testing whether the surface still holds us.

sentences that became accusations,
apologies stuck like fishbones in our throats.

happening here, in this room,
where we sit like pieces of a game

the way hunted animals learn

to become invisible,
to hope the footsteps pass.

item.topic: an elderly couple eating soup in silence

haiku_bare.text:
The spoons click against ceramic.
Steam rises, then nothing.
Their hands move without thinking—
lift, blow, sip.
She salts her portion.
He doesn't glance over.
The soup cools.
They don't hurry.
Outside: traffic, a siren, neighbors' voices.
Inside: the small sounds of eating.
His jaw works methodically.
Her bread dips once into the bowl.
Ten thousand meals like this.
Maybe more.
Maybe less.
Neither of them counting.

haiku_cascade.text:
Spoon meets bowl meets mouth.
No music. No soft lighting in memory.
Just the mechanics of swallowing,
her hand trembling slightly,
his spoon scraping the bottom.

Sixty years and they haven't learned
to call it communion.
The steam rises. Falls.
The silence is not profound—
it's the silence of a parking lot,
full of separate objects.

She calculates the thermostat.
He's already forgotten what he meant to say.

The soup cools.
Their shoulders don't touch.
The bowl reflects nothing back.

meta.surface_draft:

The soup cools.

meta.surface_revision:
Spoon meets bowl meets mouth.
No music. No soft lighting in memory.
Just the mechanics of swallowing,
her hand trembling at half-second intervals,
his spoon scraping the bottom.

Sixty years and they haven't learned
to call it communion.
The steam rises. Falls.
The silence is not profound—
it's the silence of a parking lot,
full of separate objects.

She calculates the thermostat.
He's already forgotten what he meant to say.

The soup reaches a temperature neither of them will acknowledge.
Their shoulders don't touch.
The bowl reflects nothing back.

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
They have synchronized their spoons
to the half-second, their swallows
to a shared silence that isn't silence
but its perfect opposite—
a language of throat and bowl.

Thirty years of not looking.
Thirty years of this.

The soup cools. They eat it anyway.
The sound of their eating fills the room
like weather—unavoidable, specific, unnamed.

When her hand trembles, his does not.
When his does not, hers continues steadily.

They have learned to be two
people eating soup
without the fiction of togetherness.

No touch. No glance.
Just the small violence of spoons,
the betrayal of hunger
still present, still insisting
in their aging mouths.

people eating soup
without the fiction of togetherness.

No touch. No glance.
Just the small violence of spoons,
the betrayal of hunger
still present, still insisting
in their aging mouths.

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
She adds salt. He doesn't.
The radio talks about cold. Neither listens.
His spoon scrapes the bowl's bottom before hers is half-gone.
He continues anyway. The small sound of his swallow
as the spoon moves through cooling broth.
Her hand, spotted, lies on the table.
His hand, shaking, lies on the table's edge.
She sets her spoon flat. He leaves an inch.
Possibly Tuesdays are always like this.
Possibly it doesn't matter what day it is.
The light above them is steady now.
She's stopped noticing when it's not.
By next week, the soup will be forgotten.
The tremor in his hand will continue.

meta.surface_draft:
Soup steams. Two bowls on the table.
They lift spoons. The metal scrapes ceramic.
She adds salt to hers. He doesn't.
Each sip audible—the small sound of swallowing.
Between them, the radio talks about weather.
Neither listens.
His hand rests on the table's edge.
Her spoon moves from bowl to mouth.
The soup cools. They eat anyway.
When she finishes, she sets her spoon down flat across the bowl.
He continues. There is no rush.
The kitchen light flickers slightly.
Neither mentions it.
Her hands are spotted. His shake a little.
Soup—just soup—
the thing they do on a Tuesday or any day.
Nothing more. Nothing less.

item.topic: the moment before a phone call answers

haiku_bare.text:
# Before the Answer

The ringing holds its breath.
I hold mine.

There is a space between intention and arrival—
where my words wait, arranged like furniture
in a room no one's entered yet.

The line pulses, a heartbeat in the wires.
I listen to what hasn't been said.

In this threshold,
I am still becoming
the version of myself
your voice will meet.

The silence is not empty.
It is full of all the versions of this conversation
before one of them happens.

And then—

haiku_cascade.text:
the air holds its breath
before the voice arrives—
this ribbon of sound
suspended between
dial and answer,
between what I meant to say
and what will actually fall out.

my finger still on the phone.
the line hums,
patient as a held note,
as a door left partly open
waiting for someone to walk through.

I live in these rings,
temporary citizen of maybe,
of almost,
of all the ways this could begin.

the ringing is its own country.
I am waiting at the border.

and then: the world shifts.
someone breathes on the other end.
the threshold is crossed.
the held thing is released.

of almost,

the ringing is its own country.

someone breathes on the other end.

the held thing is released.
meta.surface_revision:
the air holds its breath
before the voice arrives—
this ribbon of sound
suspended between
dial and answer,
between what I meant to say
and what will actually spill out.

my finger still on the phone.
the line hums,
patient as a held note,
as a door left partly open
waiting for someone to walk through.

I live in these rings,
permanent resident of maybe,
native speaker of almost,
fluent in every way this could begin.

the ringing is its own country.
I am waiting at the border.

and then: the world shifts.
someone breathes on the other end.
the threshold is crossed.
the held thing becomes voice.

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Before the voice arrives—
that threshold where the phone hums its single note
and you have already made the choice to call.

It is the most ordinary suspension,
happening in cars and kitchens,
in waiting rooms and offices,
a thousand times each hour
in a thousand rooms.

Three rings. Four.
The mechanical patience of the sound,
neither eager nor reluctant,
just the plain machinery of connection
attempting itself.

You stand or sit.
The phone warm against your ear
or glowing in your palm.

What will they say?
What will happen next?

But these questions live elsewhere.
Here there is only the interval,
this common threshold,
the most familiar gap
between the intention and the answer,
the instant before the world
takes any particular shape.

between the thrown stone and the splash.

Your hand is a small temple
around the phone's warmth,
a thing held sacred or questioned.

Time moves differently here:
thick as honey, bright as glass.
The ringing continues its prayer,
and you are the gathered congregation,
poised at the threshold of answer.

All possible words live in this breath,
hover like wings against your silence—
waiting for the door to open,
for sound to become echo,
for distance to collapse
into this thin silver line
they say connects us.

Now produce the revised response."]' timed out after 120 seconds

haiku_cascade_event_gated.text:
the air holds its breath
before the voice arrives—
this ribbon of sound
suspended between
dial and answer,
between what I meant to say
and what will actually fall out.

my finger still on the phone.
the line hums,
patient as a held note,
as a door left partly open
waiting for someone to walk through.

I live in these rings,
temporary citizen of maybe,
of almost,
of all the ways this could begin.

the ringing is its own country.
I am waiting at the border.

and then: the world shifts.
someone breathes on the other end.
the threshold is crossed.
the held thing is released.
